title: How cannulas changed my practice
excerpt: And I mean just reflecting on my experience with cannulas which have been pretty revolutionary in my practice as many of you know where we now have a cannula training course specifically it's going to be then to was a few scheduled in the day over tomorrow as our first one so bee…

segments.text: and I mean just reflecting on my experience with cannulas which have been pretty revolutionary in my practice as many of you know where we now have a cannula training course specifically it's going to be then to was a few scheduled in the day over tomorrow as our first one so been thinking about what happened to me in terms of my journey with Kendall is to see if you can kind of understand wedding and where they might fit in with your practice the first thing is I wasn't trained generally by the my mentors mostly were alig and trainers Rodger Keller and David Eccleston various routes do DeMayo now as well and but they tended to in years come by to be more focused on needles certainly in their official training now I think a lot of those practitioners use Canon there's a lot more in their day to day practice and they were on the training and this kind of affected when I got involved with kenya's a little bit and but I tell you what did happen rapidly which was probably the biggest

thing is that you not you actually initially find them a bit stressful because you used to just sticking the needle in getting the job done coming out and all of a sudden there's this extra stage of placement I think a lot of people find that a bit stressful because you it's kind of fiddly it's tricky you know the longer it takes the more uncertain you think your client is feeling and they're like what's going on and why isn't this happening and why they feel messing about so it can feel a bit stressful at the start but quite rapidly I found myself finding it really difficult to use needles where I used to do all the time and the first place this happened was in the middle labial folds so back in the day that was you know we do these kind of cross hatching and it's still the fund a foundation skill which I still use occasionally day to day but I massively moved to using cannulas in that area and it's for one really key reason which is as most of you all know when you do put a needle in here there's a big kind of facial veins that run down you often see them running underneath your neck here so you can spot them before you object but you're crossing an

area where there's a vein basically and huge hematomas are quite possible and actually certainly in my early days I remember getting quite a few big ones I think I was a little bit deeper than I thought I inject now and or a little bit too superficial depending on the way your volumizing or treating increase but that those big hematomas are very unpleasant and I you know I knew you'd always know straightaway when you've caused them you know inject you see that you know you turn around you changing your needle or something and you come back and there's a this little balloon area and it's like I really do not like that feeling at all so that what happened with Kenan is I rapidly realized two things one is very good for actually the lowest part of the Mena labial fold creating a straight jawline tecna's are amazing at that because you can line them up exactly on where you want to create and strengthen the area and you can fill that and but the second thing is just way less chance of bruising so what I find myself doing is gravitating to more and more almost like on an emotional level like I don't want to put a needle in because I know that

like one in ten times I'm getting pulling out and there's a gush of blood whereas with a cannula once you've got that initial pilot hole you don't get that at all and so you start to naturally do it less and less until it actually starts to feel a bit wrong putting a needle into areas where you've been using it can know for a long time because it just works so much better it's if less bruising you get the result you want the other thing that happens over time is you become a bit quicker because you can through one hole you can sometimes place an entire mill now if you think how many individual injection points you have to use to place it you know formula filler sometimes you might be going in 10 even 20 times that needle so the bruisings through the roof it's much more traumatic there's more blood involved is more waiting in between injections as you hold the bleeder so it can really become actually very quick and if you ever see these you know people on stage and over it so tomorrow we'll do a presentation and inject a kind of 1518 syringes in a session and certainly when I'm doing big treatments like that I couldn't do with a needle you just can't like it would just it would be horrific for the

patient and it would you have tons of bruising it would actually take you a lot longer unless you've got a system there to hold each brew each bruising area you're just stopping all the time to hold areas to try and reduce bruising so this is quite an emotional twist that you suddenly think like do I have to use a needle it starts to feel like oh that is the best thing to do there so I'll use it but become you become reluctant using a needle so it's been quite a big shift for me and the other thing that there gets onto your mind is its of complications now I'm pretty sure the complication rate from blocking a blood vessel with a cannula will be 1% of what it is with a needle it just that's why I instinctively feel it's incredibly different injecting with a needle bit compared to the Canon turns your risk of getting into blood vessel so once you start a really way of the chance of complications plus the fact that you get less hematomas and you just gravitate more and more towards them so a beautiful hear other people's experiences and for me it was a game-changer but it was it

happened in a period of about four weeks so you know you do your training you try it's a bit fiddly and I really enjoy it then you have a couple of successes and then all of a sudden you find yourself unable to pick up a needle for those areas anymore because it just feels so much cleaner quicker easier less painful and it's not entirely without pain obviously cause you smoking needle in but the word people use most often is it is weird it feels weird under the skin but I think I'd rather have weird than pain so yeah I'm interested in hearing your thoughts tell me what you think about using cannulas what's your experience so far did you have you had the same experience of suddenly like going from one extreme to the other using needles to suddenly I'm only using any gonna use cannulas and there are some places where we bounce back the other way so tear trough is one of those we went straight to mainly using cannula because they were less bruising but then discovered that because it's slightly less deep you actually there are times when you need to go deeper and the needle is the best place the best way to do that so I don't not entirely only using cannulas but it's definitely been

it's probably become 70% of my dermal filler treatments so yeah interested to knowing what how everyone else got started on it because I don't think a lot of manufacturers train so they're if they're making a big impact on how other people train in the industry so they tend to use more needles that can change your perception of how often to use a cannula and but if you've made the shift if you've done training or if you're thinking about doing training what are your thoughts what do you think it's good what difference do you think you'll make to your practice what difference has it made to your practice and what are your what your philosophy now what do you say to patients we're really interesting to know so some of your views on cannula be nice to hear and yeah that's just my thought for the day so the final thing I was gonna say is if anyone else came across that case it was quite well well-known by my colleagues which was there was a case on me an AC network of someone who had a blocked blood vessel and probably part of the for orbital artery with a deep injection supposed to be deeper but anybody know for sure because no none

of us were there supposed to be a deep injection onto the nasolabial fold usually that that's at the periosteum level the artery is meant to be more superficial but clearly filler got into there blocked some of the internal blood vessels and you know there was no crisis in the nose and the palate so quite a horrible injury but that got me thinking again once again about could you've done the same job at the cannula would you've got the same result or a very similar result and not have had that risk because I'm pretty sure a gentle technique with a cannula and you can get fairly deep and penetrate a blood vessel and maybe that's a better way to do it so I'm just thinking that way we still teach that I still see needles as a bit like what print writing is to cursive writing you know you kind of need to start with the basics you need to have it as a skill set but how quickly should you be moving on to using cannulas and more areas and I think probably quite quickly because I think it's safer thank you probably if once you've got the knack of it you'll enjoy it more so yeah but those are my thoughts on Canada's interesting hear your comments what do you think and

please let me know I love reading the comments so it really helps me to kind of hone the content I'm producing so ask questions share your ideas you may completely disagree with me that's always fun so yeah let me know in the comments tag anyone else who you think might be interested in this or who would like to contribute to and I love table thanks low
